channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Containment in Philosophy and Formal Ontology
item.description:
This text collects the philosophical and formal-ontological sources behind the container concept. The core claim: containment is not parthood. Being inside something is a spatial relation, not a compositional one.

¶Aristotle: vessel as moved place

Aristotle, Physics IV.4, 212a14–20 (Hardie–Gaye translation):

“just as the vessel is transportable place, so place is a non-portable vessel.”

Aggeion (vessel) is to topos (place) as moveable is to immoveable. Both are the enclosing boundary of what they contain. Place cannot be relocated; the vessel can. The definitive formulation at 212a20: “place is the innermost motionless boundary of what contains it.” The vessel is the innermost boundary that can move — a mobile container.

item.title: Action and Change
item.description:
The wind blows a door shut. A person closes the door. In both cases the door changes state. The difference is that only the second is an action. Action is change attributed to an agent — it requires both a causal structure (something changed) and a normative structure (someone is responsible). This text surveys the traditions that built that understanding and the formal systems that encode it.

¶Philosophy of action

Anscombe’s Intention (1957) established that an action is not a special kind of bodily movement but an event under a description. The same arm-raising is a stretch, a vote, or a signal depending on what the agent knows they are doing. An action is intentional under the descriptions the agent could give if asked “why?” — descriptions that reveal practical reasoning, not mere cause. This is why the wind cannot act: not because it lacks force, but because it lacks descriptions under which it does what it does knowingly.

item.title: State, Time, and Event
item.description:
State, time, and event look like three concepts. They are one structure seen three ways.

A state is a configuration — what holds right now. An event is a transition — what happens between configurations. Time is the ordering that makes “between” meaningful. Philosophy and mathematics have each developed accounts of these concepts independently, but when you lay them side by side a single pattern emerges: a trace through a state space, where the state carries exactly enough information to determine what can happen next, and events are the steps.
